name: Cuthbertson Mountain
short_description: Rockhounding site in the Ozark region with gentle slopes ideal for collecting quartz and feldspar specimens. Popular with collectors seeking Missouri mineral samples.
description:
Cuthbertson Mountain is a modest elevation point in Iron County's mineral-rich Ozark foothills. The site sits within a region known for crystalline mineral deposits formed during the Precambrian basement complex development and subsequent weathering of Paleozoic strata overlying the Ozark Plateau.

This location offers collectors access to quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica sheets that occur in the weathered granite and metamorphic bedrock exposed along the gentle slopes. The easy terrain makes it accessible for rockhounds of varying experience levels looking to collect personal specimens typical of the Missouri Ozarks.

The area is characterized by sparse vegetation on the upper elevations transitioning to mixed forest on lower slopes. Collectors will find exposed outcrops and loose material scattered across the hillside, particularly where erosion has naturally concentrated mineral specimens. The gentle slope and elevation of 1364 feet provide comfortable hiking conditions without extreme scrambling.
history: Cuthbertson Mountain sits within the Ozark Uplift province where Precambrian igneous and metamorphic rocks form the basement beneath the younger sedimentary cover. The quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens found here originated during ancient granite intrusions and regional metamorphism roughly 1.4 billion years ago, with secondary concentrations created by weathering and erosion of these resistant minerals over millions of years.
tips: Search along the gentle hillside slopes and wash areas where water erosion has exposed and concentrated mineral specimens over time. Bring a hand lens to examine feldspar striations and mica cleavage planes - these features help confirm authentic specimens. Look for quartz with a glassy luster and milky-white to translucent appearance, and mica as thin, silvery sheets that flake naturally from larger deposits. The best collecting typically occurs after spring rains when fresh material is exposed.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 1364 feet with gentle slopes suitable for easy hiking. Mixed oak and hickory forest with some exposed rock outcrops. Terrain is uneven but not steep, with loose rock and scattered mineral debris common throughout the collecting area. Walking distance from parking area varies but typically less than one mile to productive collecting zones.
written_directions: From Ironton, take MO 21 north toward the Ozark foothills approximately 8 miles. Look for access points along county roads leading toward the Cuthbertson Mountain area in eastern Iron County. Local rockhounding guidebooks and county maps provide more detailed access routes. Contact Iron County Parks or local rockhounding clubs for current access information and parking details.
road_conditions: Varied - county roads leading to the area may be unpaved; passenger vehicles suitable for main approach but final access may require off-road parking
parking_notes: Limited informal parking along county roads near the collecting area. No developed parking lot; use pullouts along access roads
collection_rules: Personal use only. Respect private property boundaries - verify public access before collecting. No motorized equipment or excavation permitted. Take only what you can reasonably carry. Follow Leave No Trace principles and fill any holes created during collecting.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment or heavy excavation
Respect private property boundaries
Fill holes created during collecting
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Ironton, approximately 8 miles south - gas, food, and lodging available
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Uneven rocky ground and loose material requires sturdy footwear but no steep scrambling. Suitable for children aged 6 and older with supervision. Not wheelchair accessible due to natural terrain.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Cuthbertson Mountain in Missouri?
faq.answer: Cuthbertson Mountain is known for quartz, feldspar, mica, and jasper specimens. The quartz typically appears as milky-white to translucent crystals, while feldspar shows characteristic striations. Mica occurs as silvery, thin sheets that naturally flake from larger deposits. These minerals reflect the Precambrian granite and metamorphic bedrock underlying the Ozark region.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Cuthbertson Mountain free, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Cuthbertson Mountain is free and requires no permit for personal use collection. However, you must verify public access before collecting and respect private property boundaries in the area. Commercial collection is not permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time to rockhound near Ironton at Cuthbertson Mountain?
faq.answer: The best seasons are April through May and September through November. These periods offer comfortable temperatures and lower humidity compared to summer months. Spring rains help expose fresh mineral specimens, making April and May particularly productive for collectors.

faq.question: Is Cuthbertson Mountain rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Cuthbertson Mountain is very beginner-friendly with easy terrain and gentle slopes requiring minimal hiking experience. The site is suitable for families with children aged 6 and older. The accessible difficulty level and straightforward specimen identification make it ideal for introducing new rockhounds to mineral collecting.

faq.question: What tools and gear do you need for rockhounding at Cuthbertson Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens or magnifying glass to examine feldspar striations and mica cleavage patterns. A small hammer and chisel help extract specimens carefully, though many loose pieces can be found without tools. Wear sturdy hiking boots for the rocky terrain, bring water, and pack a backpack to carry specimens. Consider bringing a GPS device since cell service is spotty.

faq.question: How far is Cuthbertson Mountain from Ironton, and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: Cuthbertson Mountain is approximately 8 miles north of Ironton, Missouri. Ironton offers gas stations, food, lodging, and supplies before heading out to collect. The area experiences spotty cell service, so plan ahead and let someone know where you are going.

meta_description: Free public rockhounding at Cuthbertson Mountain, Missouri. Collect quartz, feldspar, and mica. Easy terrain, beginner-friendly, no permit required.
